info.topictitle: R1.2.3 Standard enthalpy changes of combustion and formation (Higher Level Only)

markdown:
## Standard Enthalpy of Combustion ($őĒH_c^\circ$) and Formation ($őĒH_f^\circ$)


## Standard Enthalpy of Combustion ($őĒH_c^\circ$)


<definition term="Standard enthalpy of combustion">The **standard enthalpy of combustion **($őĒH_c^\circ$) is the energy change when one mole of a substance is completely burned in oxygen under standard conditions (298 K and 1 atm).</definition>

The products of combustion must also be in their **standard **states (e.g., CO‚āā as a gas and H‚āāO as a liquid).

<callout type="example">The combustion of methane (CH‚āĄ) is represented as:

$$\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g) \to \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2O(l) \quad \Delta H_c^\circ = -890 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

This equation tells us that burning 1 mole of methane releases 890 kJ of energy.</callout>

### Key Features of $őĒH_c^\circ$


1. **Exothermic Nature**: Combustion reactions are always **exothermic**, meaning $őĒH_c^\circ$ values are negative because energy is released to the surroundings.
2. **Standard States**: All reactants and products must be in their standard states. 
3. **Applications**: $őĒH_c^\circ$ values are essential for comparing the energy efficiency of different fuels, such as coal, gasoline, and hydrogen.

<callout type="example">Oxygen is a gas (O‚āā(g)), and water is a liquid (H‚āāO(l)) at standard conditions.</callout>

<callout type="example">#### **Combustion of Butane**


The combustion of butane (C‚āĄH‚āĀ‚āÄ), a fuel commonly used in lighters, is represented as:

$$\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}(g) + \frac{13}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \to 4\text{CO}_2(g) + 5\text{H}_2O(l) \quad \Delta H_c^\circ = -2878 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

This means burning 1 mole of butane releases 2878 kJ of energy.</callout>

<callout type="tip">* $őĒH_c$ values for common substances are listed in **Section 14** of the IB Chemistry Data Booklet. 
* Use these values for accurate calculations.</callout>

## Standard Enthalpy of Formation ($őĒH_f^\circ$)


<definition term="Standard enthalpy of formation">The **standard enthalpy of formation **($őĒH_f^\circ$) is the energy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its elements in their standard states under standard conditions.</definition>

<callout type="example">The formation of water (H‚āāO) from hydrogen and oxygen is:

$$\text{H}_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \to \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \quad \Delta H_f^\circ = -286 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

This means that forming 1 mole of liquid water from hydrogen gas and oxygen gas releases 286 kJ of energy.</callout>

### Key Features of $őĒH_f^\circ$


1. **Reference Point**: The $őĒH_f^\circ$ of any **element in its standard state **is **zero**. 
2. **Exothermic or Endothermic: Formation reactions can be either exothermic (negative $\Delta H_f^\circ$) or endothermic (positive $\Delta H_f^\circ$), depending on the compound.**
3. **Applications**: $őĒH_f^\circ$ values are used to calculate reaction enthalpy changes using **Hess‚Äôs law**.

<callout type="example">* $őĒH_f^\circ$ of O‚āā(g) = 0
    * $őĒH_f^\circ$ of C(s, graphite) = 0</callout>

<callout type="example">#### **Formation of Methane**


The formation of methane (CH‚āĄ) from carbon and hydrogen is represented as:

$$\text{C}(s) + 2\text{H}_2(g) \to \text{CH}_4(g) \quad \Delta H_f^\circ = -74.8 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

This means forming 1 mole of methane releases 74.8 kJ of energy.</callout>

<callout type="note">$őĒH_f^\circ$ values for many compounds are listed in** Section 13** of the IB Chemistry Data Booklet.</callout>
